title: INCREASE Housing Affordability Act
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Financial Services, for a period to be
plain_summary: This bill helps people turn empty office buildings into homes by giving them money back on their taxes. For every dollar someone spends converting an office building into apartments or houses, they get 15 cents back from the government. They can get even more money back if they create affordable housing or pay workers fair wages. The bill also sets up a group of experts to help local governments figure out which old office buildings would make good homes and how to get permission to change them.
last_action_text: ASSUMING FIRST SPONSORHSIP - Mr. Magaziner asked unanimous consent that he may be hereafter be considered as the first sponsor of H.R. 537, a bill originally introduced by Representative Sherrill, for
subjects: Urban and suburban affairs and development

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Incentivizing New Conversions to Residential Entities to Accelerate Supply and Expand Housing Affordability Act or the INCREASE Housing Affordability Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Commercial-to-Residential Credit
sections.text: This section changes the tax code to create a new tax credit for people who convert commercial buildings into homes. The credit equals fifteen percent of the money spent on converting the building. However, there are limits on how much credit you can get. You cannot get more than two hundred thousand dollars for each new housing unit you create. You also cannot get more than ten million dollars for each building you convert. To get the credit, you must spend the money to convert an old office building that was built at least fifteen years ago into apartments or homes. The building must have been used for offices before the conversion. You can get extra credit if you make some of the housing affordable for people with lower incomes. You can also get extra credit if you pay workers fair wages during the conversion. The amount of extra credit depends on how affordable the housing is and ranges from ten percent to twenty percent more. If you pay workers fair wages, you get fifteen percent extra credit. You cannot use this credit for the cost of buying the building, only for the cost of converting it. You also cannot use this credit if you already got other housing tax credits for the same expenses. There are detailed rules about when you can claim the credit and what expenses count. These changes apply to conversion expenses that happen after this law is passed.

sections.title: Section 3  Commercial to Residential Conversion Advisory Board
sections.text: The Secretary of Housing and Urban Development must create an advisory board within one year of this law being passed. This board will have at least twenty members chosen by the Secretary. The board will help state and local housing agencies figure out how to convert commercial buildings into homes. The board will help agencies identify which commercial buildings would be good candidates for conversion based on whether the conversion makes financial sense and whether there is demand for housing in that area. The board will also help with floor plan studies and feasibility studies for potential conversions. The board will provide support to speed up government approval processes and construction permits so conversions can happen faster. The board will also help reform local rules and zoning laws that make conversions difficult. Additionally, the board will help identify federal and state funding sources that local governments can use to help pay for conversion projects. Congress authorizes five million dollars to be spent each year from twenty twenty five through twenty twenty nine to fund this advisory board.
